speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about Maribor, Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And the smuggler brought us to a large building on the outskirts of the town. And a door opened. And we found us in a courtyard. And a man [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was waiting for us. And his name-- I will also never forget-- was Marcus Rosner.

speaker: interviewer
text: R-O--

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-S-N-E-R. Marcus Rosner. And a Yugoslavian Jew, living in Maribor, which, out of the goodness of his heart, took in all the Jews crossing over from Austria. And not only Austrian Jews. There were German Jews, some from other parts of Yugoslavia. Took them in and send them on the way to Zagreb. Because Maribor was too close to the border to stay. And the Jewish community of Maribor was too small. They couldn't absorb that many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived in this house with the courtyard, did you get any water and food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Yeah. I must say, he really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] made us as comfortable as he could. A matter of fact, I remember we washed. We were very, very dirty.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was alone? Marcus Rosner?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He had a wife. And he had a large family. I don't remember anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they helped as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: They helped as well. Either they were his servants or were his children. I really don't know. I was only there very short time, few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: We freshed up. We got food. And he rented taxis. And in groups of four or five, we were brought from Maribor, quite a distance, to Zagreb, and to the Jewish community, to the Israeli Center of Zagreb. And, of course, they knew already from our coming. Apparently they were-- you know, he notified them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they knew already. And they had a very good system of taking care of refugees. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had a lot of poor Jewish Yugoslav families living in Zagreb, under the help of the Jewish community. Were they Yugoslavian Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm talking about Yugoslavian Jews. So they told them, we give you a family. Or we give you two, three refugees. You put them up in one of your rooms. And you provide minimal breakfast and food. And we pay you. And this way, my mother and I, we got a shelter in Opatovina, [NON-ENGLISH], that is Opatovina Street in the upper part of Zagreb, with an older Jewish couple. And also, we had no difficulties in speaking with them because they spoke German.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This part of Yugoslavia's Zagreb, and even Belgrade, was Austrian territory before the First World War. Even the First World War started in Sarajevo, if you remember. And, of course, those old people, they went to Austrian schools, and German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was their language at that time. Perhaps not the first language, but their second language.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had no difficulty in speaking with them. And, also, Rosner spoke [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] German. But, for example, the taxi driver, we couldn't converse with him because we couldn't understand him. What section of Yugoslavia? What province was this Zagreb? Was this Slovenia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Maribor is Slovenia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's located in Slovenia. Zagreb is located in Croatia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Croatia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on we found out that the biggest anti-Semites in Yugoslavia are the Ustase, in Zagreb. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If I could back up one moment. I mentioned Otto Perl, a cousin of mine, came back from Dachau. And his parents bought him the ticket to go to England. And he was interned in England, on the Isle of Man. But his parents didn't survive. And I was still in Vienna when my Aunt Marta Perl committed suicide with gas in their kitchen, on the day she got the notification of her being picked up for the Poland transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the next day, when the truck arrived to pick them up, my uncle, Paul Perl, Otto's father, had a heart attack and died [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the moment when he had to enter the truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the street. He was laying on the street for a day, day and a half, till the [NON-ENGLISH], the Jewish burial society, picked him up and gave him a burial. Because the Austrian didn't really care.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was when I still was in Vienna. I forgot to mention it before. I think I should have mentioned it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on with the taxis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I say, the taxis arrived in Zagreb, in intervals. Rosner didn't send them out together. He sent them out in hourly intervals. And all the Jews which crossed the border were brought to Zagreb and were accommodated same way like we were. None of them had any money. Most of them had no clothes, other than they could carry. And I must say the Yugoslav community took very good care of us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: A few days later, I met Joschko Indig. Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried to find a similar group like I had in Vienna, with Aron Menczer, also in Zagreb. Which means I wanted to be with the same age group of people. And I wanted to-- I didn't know anybody. I couldn't speak the language. I had no money. So the only way of entertainment, or of being with people of the same thinking, was with a youth group. And I went to the Israeli [INAUDIBLE] command. And they told me here is an house where the Jewish children congregate. And the Jewish children had then a person which is called [NON-ENGLISH]. That is the leader of a group. And the leader of the group at the time was Joschko Indig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for us, Robert? Joschko?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joschko is J-O-S-C-H-K-O. Indig is I-N-D-I-G-- Indig.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was Yugoslavian?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Yugoslavian student. He was not much taller than me. He had a pronounced, long nose, I remember. And he spoke fluently German. Later on I found out he came from a very religious background. His parents, his father and his mother, were Orthodox Jews. And he may have rebelled a little bit, because he joined the Hashomer Hatzair, which is a leftish youth group. And then he saw the necessity of helping the children coming from Austria and Germany. He started to bring them together. The Jewish community in Zagreb gave them a building. And most of the orphans actually lived in this building. They had their beds there. They had their kitchen facilities and so on. And he started a small school. The studies were more Jewish and Palestine directed than in regular schools. I remember I wrote the first time I ever write, the language of Israel today, spoken and taught from Joschko Indig in Zagreb. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What he did is actually very similar what Aron Menczer did in Vienna. He congregated all the children. He tried to occupy them during the day. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he gave his time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: For the adults, was a different story. They had different interests. And we were not very much interested in, you know, that life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know? What were Joschko Indig's plans for all these children? Was there enough? Did more people keep coming to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joschko Indig, the Jewish community-- Racha Fryer, which was a German woman-- they tried to collect all the children coming the pipeline down from Germany and Austria, sent by Aron Menczer to [? Schleich, ?] to [? Graz, ?] [? Speilberg, ?] [? Strauss, ?] Maribor, Rosner, to Zagreb.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, there was still freedom in Yugoslavia. Because the Germans occupied Yugoslavia in April of 1941 and I arrived in the late part of November. You know? 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: '40. So we had four months of freedom in Yugoslavia. During those four months, transports were organized from the Yugoslavian Jewish community. Which means they rented all the ships on the Danube. By ships, please don't think big ships. I meant very small paddle wheelers which went down the Danube to the Black Sea, made their way to the Bosphorus, to Turkey, and finally tried to get to Palestine, which was under the British mandate. And they had a terrible policy of holding up the Jews. But a few ships made it. And the Jewish community, Aron Menczer-- Joschko Indig, they tried to organize transports. And they concentrated, let's say, 100 children or 20 adults, a little boat, a little ship, a willing captain with lot of money. That was not cheap. They Didn't do it out of the goodness of their hearts. And in matter of fact, the last transport of 94 children, I was still there when they went to Belgrade and to the boat on the Danube. And every one of those 94 children made it to Palestine. I met them in Palestine. They were very active in the creation of the state of Israel. Unfortunately, this open door closed on the day Germany occupied Yugoslavia, in April of '41. From that moment on, there was no escape going down the Danube. Because the war started with Greece, Turkey, and this opening closed. I forgot to tell you too, in the four months period of time of liberty, my mother and I were able to get in contact with my father in South America. My father died shortly thereafter, in 1941. But at that time, we got, I believe, two letters from him back. And, of course, that closed too when Germany occupied Yugoslavia. The mail didn't exist anymore. But we had at least twice the occasion to get a letter. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] With the German occupation of Yugoslavia, our life changed too because now the persecution of the Jews start. And when the Yugoslavian Jews were in this entrapment, and were under very bad conditions, you can imagine how we were. We were there without any money and without any papers and without any support at all. So the only support I had was actually this little door opened, which was helper Joschko Indig, in his little group. In the meantime, we were close to 40 children, which he couldn't manage to get out anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many adults were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were eight or 10 adults, including my mother. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one of the adults by the name of Mr. Avien was the cook, for example. I don't want to say he was a real cook. But he cooked for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Zagreb?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Zagreb. And my mother, took over the-- they had a sewing machine. And they had girls there too, teenager, girls. So they took care of our clothes. They washed our clothes. They ironed our clothes. They mended our clothes. They-- how's this called-- made sweaters and things like that. So my mother was in charge of this little group. The situation was getting worse daily. The Ustase was getting very pronounced anti-Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Groups were taken. 300 Yugoslavian Jews were taken in the age group from, let's say, 16 to 24, and were brought to a camp and just-- we heard later-- are massacred. And we were still there and heard about things like that. Jasenovac was one of those--

speaker: interviewer
text: Camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: --terrible camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Joschko had now 40 children, eight or 10 adults. Money was very short and running out. Because now the rich Yugoslavian Jews tried to escape left and right. They tried to escape to Hungary because they thought perhaps in Hungary, their situation's a little bit better. And they were not willing to support us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not able. I shouldn't say willing. They most probably wanted to. But they were not able to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So something else happened at the time, that the Germans were very much occupied with the war in Greece. The war with Russia started in September of 1941. Troops had to be diverted. So they started to split up Yugoslavia. By splitting up, I mean they made an Italian-occupied area and a German-occupied area. And the Italian-occupied area was in Slovenia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Jews have a very easy way of getting to know things. And the Italians were better to the Jews than the Germans. So what happened is we tried to go to the Italian-occupied area. And Joschko got in contact with the Yugoslavian Jews in Lubiana. And, in matter of fact, the name of one I will never forget was Bolaffi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-O-L-A-F-F-I. I just found out that the father of Bolaffi was a very famous painter. And both brother Bolaffis, one was Eugene and one was-- don't remember it right now-- they were wine merchants, very-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very rich wine merchants in Lubiana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish. And out of mitzvah and out of their good heart, they were able to rent [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a house by the name of Grad Lesno Brdo.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're going to have to spell that for us, Robert.

speaker: interviewee
text: G-R-A-D, L-E-S-N-O, B-R-D-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say it again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grad Lesno Brdo. In the vicinity of Horjul-- H-O-R-J-U-L-- perhaps 15 to 20 kilometers in the east of Lubiana. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was an empty house. And he rented it for us. And Joschko told us the only way I can do it, I bring you from Zagreb to Lubiana. At that time, the railway existed. And we just went on. We had only the papers to show that we had a rental house in Grad Lesno Brdo. We had no travel papers at all. But in the meantime, the group of 42, 48 children and the 10 adults, the children were in an age group from 8 to 17. And most of them, they're now orphans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day we traveled. And we made it to Lubiana. And on the border crossing, the Italian troops, they were very friendly to us. And the moment they heard orphan, oh, they didn't care what papers. You said, the border crossing. This border was between--

speaker: interviewee
text: The border was between German-occupied-- that was not really a border anymore. That was more a military zone. It was the military zone between German-occupied areas-- because Yugoslavia was occupied by German-- and Italian-occupied areas. And we made it to Lesno Brdo. And in Lesno Brdo, Joschko was able to continue the idea of [NON-ENGLISH], which means preparation to the life of a person in Palestine, in a kibbutz. And he did it with the help of a few people, like Mr. Avien. He was our cook. He was also our cook in Lesno Brdo. With the help of my mother, which took care of laundry, clothes, and so on. With the help of a German professor by the name of Dr. Boris Jochwitson. And a German Jew. And he was a pianist by profession. But he had a vast knowledge in many, many things. And Marco was another adult, which was in charge of buying food and bringing stuff to us. And a few other ones which helped in the group. And he started classes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had regular school classes, actually, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: This Lesno Brdo was a house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: A manor house?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small manor house. It was empty for many years. And it was used previously-- and I'm talking about now perhaps 80 years back when the Austrian-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before the First World War, when they had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] royalty. It was used by them as a, you know, location. But for us, it was godsend. And we had, even, a little garden there, where vegetables were grown.

speaker: interviewer
text: You arrived Grad Les-- no, I'm sorry. I can't pronounce it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lesno Brdo.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in what month of 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: It may have been August.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would have stayed there for the end of the war, till the end of the war. Unfortunately, the Yugoslav people started to revolt against the German occupation. The partisans started to become very active, under Tito. And in our area, the partisan group was under the command of a Yugoslavian by the name of Cernij.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's hold the rest of that history until we get to another tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
